title: From Frustrated Mom to Political Candidate: When Parental Rights Meet Leadership
guestBio: Kristina Irwin is a mother, first-generation immigrant, and candidate for California State Senate District 24. She earned 6,300 write-in votes in 2022 after being awakened to educational concerns during COVID lockdowns and is now running on the official 2026 ballot on a platform of parental rights, property rights, and public safety.
excerpt: When COVID lockdowns revealed what was really happening in her children's classrooms, this mom didn't just complain—she decided to run for state senate. Kristina Irwin's journey from frustrated parent to political candidate offers a powerful blueprint for any mother ready to turn her mama bear instincts into meaningful change.
metaDescription: Mom turns COVID education frustrations into CA State Senate run. Kristina Irwin's journey from write-in candidate to 2026 official ballot.
keyTakeaways: Motherhood provides a masterclass in leadership skills that translate directly to political and community leadership.
Parents have the right and responsibility to know what their children are being taught in schools.
Write-in campaigns can be surprisingly effective when you have genuine grassroots support and a clear message.
Education reform should focus on teaching practical skills like AI literacy and real-world preparation rather than ideology.
Breaking political supermajorities requires common sense candidates willing to put principles over party politics.
When you feel powerless about your children's future, channel that frustration into actionable change rather than helpless anger.
Parental rights issues cross party lines and represent a fundamental women's issue about protecting our children.

pullQuotes.text: Motherhood is a masterclass in leadership

timestamps.label: What she saw her kids being taught
The Palisades fire destroys her kids' school
Motherhood as a masterclass in leadership

content:
What happens when a mom gets fed up with what her kids are learning in school? If you're Kristina Irwin, you don't just complain at PTA meetings—you run for California State Senate. In this powerful episode of The Manage Her, Aimee sits down with Kristina to explore her journey from frustrated parent to political candidate, and why her story matters for every mother who's ever felt powerless about her children's future.

The COVID Wake-Up Call That Changed Everything

Like millions of parents across America, Kristina's eyes were opened during COVID lockdowns when she could suddenly see and hear what was happening in her children's virtual classrooms. What she discovered wasn't just disappointing—it was infuriating. The curriculum wasn't focused on academics but on ideology, and she realized that what she'd assumed was education had become something entirely different.

This wasn't a gradual realization or a philosophical debate. This was a mother watching her children's minds being shaped in ways that contradicted her family's values, and she wasn't going to stand for it. That anger, that protective mama bear instinct, became the foundation for something much bigger than she initially imagined.

From Write-In Candidate to Official Ballot

Most people who get frustrated with politics complain on social media or maybe attend a school board meeting. Kristina decided to run for office—as a write-in candidate, no less. In a political landscape where name recognition and party backing seem essential, she managed to earn 6,300 write-in votes. Think about that for a moment. Six thousand three hundred people took the time to write in her name because they believed in her message and her mission.

That grassroots support didn't happen by accident. It happened because Kristina tapped into something authentic—the frustration that countless parents were feeling but didn't know how to channel productively. She gave them a voice and a choice, and they responded by literally writing her name on their ballots.

Now she's running again in 2026, but this time she'll be on the official ballot for California State Senate District 24. The write-in campaign was proof of concept. The official campaign is about creating real change.

When Disaster Strikes: Leadership Under Pressure

During their conversation, Kristina shares the devastating impact of the Palisades fire on her children's school community. Watching her talk about the destruction and the road to recovery reveals something crucial about her leadership style. She doesn't just see problems—she sees solutions and pathways forward. Even in the face of literal destruction, she's focused on how to rebuild better, stronger, and more aligned with what children actually need.

This crisis response mindset isn't something you can fake or learn from a textbook. It comes from years of motherhood, where you're constantly managing mini-crises, making quick decisions with incomplete information, and somehow keeping everyone fed, clothed, and emotionally stable while doing it.

Motherhood as Leadership Training

One of the most compelling parts of this conversation is when Kristina explains how motherhood serves as a masterclass in leadership. Think about what mothers do every single day: project management, conflict resolution, resource allocation, strategic planning, crisis management, and stakeholder relations (even if those stakeholders are arguing about who gets the last piece of pizza).

You're already leading, mama. You're already making decisions that impact multiple people's lives. You're already managing budgets, timelines, and competing priorities. The question isn't whether you have leadership skills—it's whether you're ready to use those skills beyond your household.

The Real Issues: Parental Rights and Common Sense

Kristina's platform isn't about partisan politics—it's about common sense policies that protect families. She talks about concerning legislation like AB 495, which she calls the "kidnapping bill" because of how it impacts parental rights. These aren't abstract political issues when you're a mother. These are policies that directly affect your ability to protect and guide your own children.

The conversation also covers Prop 36, California's successful ballot measure to "make crime criminal again." The fact that this passed shows that voters—regardless of party affiliation—want practical solutions to real problems. They want their communities to be safe. They want their children to be educated, not indoctrinated. They want their parental rights respected.

Education for the Real World

Instead of just criticizing the current system, Kristina offers a vision for what education could look like. She talks about teaching children to use AI as a tool, focusing on practical skills that will serve them in the real world, and getting back to actual learning rather than ideological programming. Imagine schools that prepared your children for the future instead of trying to reshape their values.

This isn't about politics—it's about preparing the next generation for success in a rapidly changing world. It's about giving them tools instead of talking points, skills instead of slogans.

Your Frustration Is Valid—Now What?

If you're listening to this and thinking, "I feel the same way but I could never run for office," Kristina has a message for you. Your frustration is valid. Your concerns are legitimate. And you have more power than you think you do. Maybe running for office isn't your path, but there are school boards to join, communities to organize, and voices to raise.

The question isn't whether you're qualified—the question is whether you're willing. Democracy works best when regular people step up to serve, not when we leave everything to career politicians who may have forgotten what it's like to worry about your child's future.

Kristina Irwin's story is proof that one frustrated mom can make a difference. She's not waiting for someone else to fix the problems she sees—she's stepping up to be part of the solution. The real question is: what are you going to do with your own mama bear energy?

quiz.title: What's Your Advocacy Style?
quiz.description: When something matters deeply to you and your family, how do you naturally step into action? Discover your unique approach to creating change.

questions.question: When you first notice a problem that affects your family or community, what's your natural first response?

options.text: I immediately start researching and want to understand exactly what's happening and who's responsible
I reach out to friends and neighbors to see if they're experiencing the same thing
I start mapping out potential solutions and thinking through the most effective approach
I look for ways to connect with others who share my concerns and might want to take action together

questions.question: You're at a meeting where everyone seems to agree on something you strongly disagree with. How do you typically handle this?

options.text: I speak up clearly about my position, even if it makes others uncomfortable
I ask thoughtful questions to understand their perspective while gently introducing my concerns
I prepare my points carefully and present a well-reasoned alternative viewpoint
I try to find others who might share my concerns so we can present a united voice

questions.question: When facing a challenge that feels bigger than what you can handle alone, you tend to:

options.text: Double down on your own efforts and push harder, trusting your instincts over others' advice
Seek out mentors and advisors who can guide you toward the best path forward
Step back to analyze the situation and develop a comprehensive strategy before moving forward
Rally others to join you because you know collective action is more powerful than going it alone

questions.question: Your ideal way to create lasting change involves:

options.text: Taking bold, decisive action that shows others it's possible to stand up for what's right
Building relationships and finding common ground that brings former opponents to your side
Developing well-researched solutions that address root causes rather than just symptoms
Empowering everyday people to see their own power and take action in their communities

questions.question: When people describe your approach to leadership challenges, they're most likely to say:

options.text: You're fearless and willing to take on fights that others avoid
You're diplomatic and skilled at bringing out the best in difficult situations
You're thorough and always seem to have thought through all the angles
You're inspiring and have a gift for helping others see their own potential

questions.question: The thing that frustrates you most about how change typically happens is:

options.text: Too many people are afraid to take a stand when it really matters
People get stuck in us-versus-them thinking instead of finding solutions together
Most approaches are reactive and short-sighted rather than addressing underlying issues
Ordinary people don't realize how much power they actually have to make a difference

results.title: The Fierce Protector
results.description: You're driven by a deep instinct to shield what matters most to you. When your values or loved ones are threatened, you don't hesitate to take bold action, even if it means going against the grain. Your protective energy is your superpower, but sometimes you might feel isolated in your intensity. This episode will show you how to channel that fierce protective instinct into strategic leadership that builds lasting change.

results.title: The Bridge Builder
results.description: You naturally see multiple perspectives and excel at bringing people together around shared values. You believe change happens through connection and understanding, not conflict. Your diplomatic approach is invaluable, though you might sometimes feel frustrated by how slowly things move. Kristina's story will inspire you with concrete ways to unite people across differences while still standing firm in your convictions.

results.title: The Strategic Planner
results.description: You approach challenges with careful analysis and methodical action. You believe in doing your homework, building coalitions, and working within systems to create sustainable change. Your thoughtful approach prevents costly mistakes, though you might sometimes feel paralyzed by wanting the perfect plan. This episode will energize you with a real example of how strategic thinking combined with bold action can break through seemingly impossible barriers.

results.title: The Community Catalyst
results.description: You instinctively understand that real change happens from the ground up. You're energized by grassroots movements and believe in the power of ordinary people coming together. Your ability to mobilize others is remarkable, though you might sometimes doubt whether your efforts are big enough to matter. Kristina's journey from frustrated mom to serious political candidate will show you exactly how grassroots energy can create extraordinary results.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Welcome back to the Manager Podcast, where we talk about real leadership women live every day, the kind that starts at home and ripples into the world. Today's episode feels especially personal to me because I'm sitting down with another mom who reached a moment many of us recognize, that point where you look at what your kids are experiencing and something inside you just says, I can't just watch this happen. I need to be part of the change.

My guest today is Kristina Irwin, a mother, advocate, and candidate California State Senate District 24. Her journey into public life didn't come from politics first. It came from motherhood, from watching her children navigate a changing education system, and from asking bigger questions about accountability, leadership, and the future we're creating for our families. Today is not about having a debate. We're having a conversation, a real one, about parenting, courage, education, and what happens when moms decide to step forward instead of staying quiet. Kristina, I'm so happy to have you here today.

**Kristina:** Thank you, Aimee. I'm so happy to be here today. Thank you. It's so nice to have you here.

**Aimee:** So I think so many moms had a moment over the last few years where we looked at our kids' education and thought, wait, something feels different. What was the moment like for you personally?

**Kristina:** For me personally, it was the usual silver lining, COVID. When it first happened, I was first to say, okay, two weeks, let's give it a shot. Nobody knows what this is, this is weird, just in case. So we waited. And into the third week, I'm like, you know what? This doesn't feel right. Something's going on. I'm gonna start researching.

So obviously I started researching. I started calling my other mom friends. And I was like, this is just so weird. And then that led into obviously the school shutdowns, the mandated don't go out in the park, don't go on the beach alone, stay home, six feet distance, all that nonsense. And so then when the school shut down, I saw what my kids were finally being taught.

And it wasn't the usual that every mom thinks, okay, you send your kid off to school and they're going to be studying English and science and history and math. It was the total far left woke nonsense that I had no idea was going on - gender ideology and race baiting and just horrible things, Fauci on there saying, oh, go ahead, get the shot, don't listen to your parents kind of stuff. And so I was like, what is happening?

And so I just decided, okay, what can I do where I could actually make a difference? So I decided to go to the top and try my hand at running for a state senator, which would be at the legislative level writing these bills and curtailing the curriculum and actually seeing what our kids are doing and making a change from that side.

**Aimee:** That's amazing. Yeah, that's such a big move to go from being a mom stepping straight into politics. It's really big. So what did the transition feel like for you emotionally?

**Kristina:** I was pissed. It's like, I'm okay with things. But then when you come after our kids in a way where it feels like they were sneaking it in? That's where I draw the line. And so I remember I was so mad. My kids were like, what's happening? I'm like, I'm going to make this change. This isn't right.

And so I remember getting on the phone with the Maha moms before we were even Maha moms, before we were known as that, we were just like don't mess with our kids, mama bears. And we got fired up. And so I got so much support that when I did run, I got a lot of write-in votes and I had to do write-in because that's a whole other story, but basically that was the year that they were redistricting.

So it wasn't what they're doing now. It was a different kind of gerrymandering, it was redistricting for state Senate. So the people, I got enough signatures, but the people whose signatures I got thought they were registered at one address, maybe their old house, their parents house, whatever it was, they basically fell off the voter rolls. Some were registered from Republican to Democrat. It was just really weird what happened. So I was five...

**Aimee:** What year is this?

**Kristina:** This was 2021 coming into 2022. So I had no choice, either wait the four years or run as a write-in, which nobody ever wins. Nobody has ever gotten more votes than I think two or 300 at that time. And I got, I was lucky enough to get 6,000, almost 400.

**Aimee:** Wow. It's crazy. Was it enough to win or what happened with that race?

**Kristina:** So that was the primary in June. That was enough to get me propelled into the general election in November. So in June, it was just my opponent, Ben Allen, at the time, and a little box that said write in and you have to spell the name correctly. And that's where people, 6,300 something, wrote in my name. And that propelled me to be on the ballot in November, head to head against Ben Allen.

**Aimee:** Wow. What does a campaign like that look like? I mean, you're a mom, you're not a politician. How do you go from zero to state Senate? What does that look like? How do you prep for that?

**Kristina:** That was a lot of just, it was completely 100% organic grassroots mama bear driven, just phone calls, people coming to me going, Oh my gosh, what can I do? How do I support you? It wasn't me so much reaching out. It was just at that time, it literally felt like God was just putting his hands on my back going, just go for it. I got you. And that's what it felt like. Doors were just opening. Things were happening. Everything was aligning. So it was great. It was wonderful.

**Aimee:** Wow. That's amazing. So you just go from being a mom and navigating it all and your kids are home. And so your kids are making the shift and are they getting back into school? What did you end up doing there with that?

**Kristina:** Yeah. So we just were Zoom schooling and they went from straight A's to when it was Zoom school to F's. So then they finally, after all that, got back into school. And I mean, now we're seeing the whole debacle happen where it's like, oh, we just gave everybody A's. It doesn't matter. I feel like our kids got left behind. I feel like they missed a good year, two years of...

And then, I mean, let me just fast forward, and especially with my kids, and now it's the Palisades fire. Our kids caught fire.

**Aimee:** Yeah, you went from one big event, life event, to another big life event. So your kids' school burned down. And they were home again. It's gone. Yeah, what do you even do? When a school burns down, where did the kids, did they figure it out? How long did it take to get your kids back in school?

**Kristina:** Yeah, so at that point, they were on Zoom again. And then I was on the board of trustees for the high school. So we voted to spend a lot of money to get them to the Sears building temporarily. This was after months already on Zoom school. I think maybe six months. I don't really remember. And then we voted to get them to go to the Sears building, which I remember going shopping there with my grandma a long time ago. And it's a defunct department store in Santa Monica, right across the street from the very dangerous metro.

So they did that. And about a month ago, they finally, everything's fine. They finally now have gone back to Pali High.

**Aimee:** Oh, my gosh. So did they, what are they doing at the high school? Did they put portables in or how is it rebuilt? What's going on over there?

**Kristina:** The pool is completely drained. So they're going to have to redo that. There's wires hanging everywhere. They're talking about not just in school, but for all of the Palisades community, having underground phone wires, which would make sense. There's a lot of porta potties. There's a lot of, I went to school at CSUN during the Northridge quakes. I mean, before the Northridge quake. So I remember it. I'm like, oh, this is familiar. You get to have a little trailer in your class. Each class is a little trailer.

**Aimee:** Great. So that's what they're experiencing. Wow. So, but at least they're in person and they're basically bootstrapping it together as far as getting the kids back into school.

**Kristina:** The school itself is doing a phenomenal job. They're great, but their hands are tied because then you have the community where the kids are in school and they feel great and they're with their friends. And then you come out to get in your friend's car or to take the bus and the whole palisades community is burned down. So it takes a toll on your psyche.

**Aimee:** Oh, yeah. I grew up going to Laguna Beach High School. We had a big fire in Laguna when I was in school. I was a freshman when that went down. And it was, the town, we lost a lot of buildings. We were lucky to keep our high school. The fire came right up to the high school, but it didn't get it. But yeah, seeing the town burnt out like that and having so many of your friends and their families be affected by it. A wildfire losing everything, losing all of their personal belongings. It's very, very sad. How did you guys fare with the wildfire? Did you guys lose anything?

**Kristina:** No, I mean, we were on evacuation warnings twice. So it's like one foot out the door, one here, one there. But constantly watching the news, constantly seeing what happened, talking to my friends. My kids' friends, everybody was communicating with everybody. So it was crazy, crazy, chaotic disaster.

**Aimee:** Wow. Yeah, that was, yeah. We have friends in the fire department that said it was really unbelievable what happened up there. Did you, when you decided, okay, I'm going to run, this is going to happen. This is what needs to happen. Did you ever have a moment where you were like, can I really do this? And the first time or this time?

**Kristina:** I guess both. I mean, I think we all faced doubt at some point, right? The first time, no. No. I was so gung-ho. I was like, nothing, come hell or high water, nothing's going to stop me. Nothing. It was literally like God was like, just, I got you. Not one doubt.

This time when I first, people were asking me, do you want to run again? I think you should. You got great numbers. Because now my kids, before they were younger and they kind of understood, now they're teenagers and it's a different kind of needy. You're like, again, mom? And so I was like a little bit of doubt, maybe 1%, but the rest is like, I got this.

**Aimee:** That is so good. Well, I mean, it's the only way that we can actually accomplish anything. It's like, you've got to push through self-doubt. You've got to have the little voice in your head that says, no, you got this. Somebody is going to win it. So why not you?

**Kristina:** That's what my mom always says. Someone's going to do it. Why not you? Exactly. Exactly. And I feel like at this point, I just don't have a choice. I have to do this, not only for my kids, but for all the other kids in the district. I know it sounds cliche, but they really are our future. I mean, first COVID, then the fires. It's like, if we don't stop this and we don't wake up the masses, it's going to keep happening. Our money is going to keep getting drained. Our souls are going to keep getting drained. So somebody with common sense has to step up. So yeah, it's me again.

**Aimee:** Oh, my gosh. So one thing I always say is that motherhood is one of the most intense leadership roles there is, even though society doesn't always see it that way. How has being a mom shaped how you lead?

**Kristina:** Delegation. I delegate. I mean, to my husband, I delegate to my oldest. Literally, he helps me the most. He's like, get in the car, guys. Do this. Do that. So it's been fun. And I think it's just it's one of those things where it's like, I know that I can't do it all myself. And I do have my little team, my family.

But I mean, then I also have my campaign team. Then I have my friends who are also not just my friends, but who are on my team and gung ho and pushing me. So it's like everywhere around me. I'm blessed. I'm loved. I can't fail. Everybody's like, just go do it. You got this.

**Aimee:** That's so amazing. And don't you find when you delegate to your team that you're empowering them to own things in ways that if we were doing things for them, they may never have that sense of ownership?

**Kristina:** Yeah, exactly.

**Aimee:** For me, I think as a working mom, that's one of the things I feel that I'm lucky that I get to empower my oldest son to be the boss of baseball and kind of help manage the younger kids and make sure they're ready to go. How does your oldest son or child help you manage your children?

**Kristina:** It started even when he's, I mean, I'm the first born, so I think it's kind of just innate, but it started with him going, you need to listen. Like she said this once. And then I'm noticing as he's getting older, he's looking at me, he's trying to give me parenting advice. He'll text me like, you really need to deal with your daughter. And I'm like, if you let her keep getting away with this. And I was like, okay, maybe I've created a little bit of a monster, but I'm grateful for it. So I kind of have to take in the reins a little bit like, Hey, Hey, Hey, Hey, who's the real boss here? Calm down.

**Aimee:** That is, I might get a little bit of that for my older kids because we have the older batch and then we have a younger batch. So we've got the three and five. And so sometimes the older batch will be like, I don't know, mom. I think, I think Indy's getting a little bossy. Or I think Paul is really getting wild at bedtime. And, you know, I don't think that so-and-so was reining him in enough. And he's like, you know, you should do it like this, X, Y, Z. And I'm like, sometimes I'm like, you know what? When you're a parent, you do it your way. But I am grateful for it because he helps and he opens my eyes. So it's good.

**Kristina:** Oh, I know. Yeah, totally. I think it's nice to have families where we work as teams. It's so nice to have that teamwork and it's so nice as a mom to feel so supported by all of the people in your house. They're not draining you. They're supporting you and loving you. And that's a really nice balanced life.

**Aimee:** It is. Yeah. So what were some of the hardest conversations you had with your kids during the school disruptions and how did those shape your perspective on education?

**Kristina:** So basically, with COVID and the same with the wildfires, it was like, basically, I try to be honest with them. I try to be truthful. They hear things at school or in the news. Oh, you know, mommy, 100 mile an hour winds. I'm like, hold on. So we'll research it. I'll tell them, I'm not going to just completely say no to your theories. But let me hear you out. And this is what I think. And this is why.

So I'm honest with them. We've talked about them, about their thoughts and about the things they're learning in school and how they're different than the thoughts that we have here at home kind of thing. So it's just, I guess, honesty, communication.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Yeah, totally. I know. I'm kind of dodged a bullet there with having my kids were in private school prior to COVID, I only had one kid who was in public and he did kindergarten and first grade in public. So he kind of missed it. It wasn't at his grade level, but my kids who were in junior high, who had friends in high school during COVID, I would hear what was being taught at public high school. And I thought, oh my goodness, I'm glad you're not there.

And so some of the stuff is just maybe inappropriate for children and then confusing to children? Is it really the place for public education to be teaching ideology versus education and academics?

**Kristina:** There's no place for that. I mean, I've said this before in many of my speeches. Public education or government education has become indoctrination. There's no place for it. I mean, whatever you as a parent want to teach your kids, that's fine. That's within your family or culture, your beliefs, your values, whatever. But when you send your kids to school, you're trusting this teacher, this campus, you're entrusting them with your children, with their education, with the safety, not for them to come back feeling asking questions that you, you as a parent aren't ready to answer, or maybe it's not in your culture.

With my parents, they never sat down and said, okay, well, let's talk about sex. It just, I'm Eastern European. It just wasn't talked about. And I couldn't imagine if I came home and I was like, you know, so today we learned about pronouns. And my parents would be like, what? Right? School is school, period.

**Aimee:** Yeah. I do think, and is there, there was barely enough time to teach us the basics, right? During those hours that we were in school, there was still a very high failure rate, even in the 80s and 90s. Do we really have time to teach ideology rather than teaching core academics? Plus, maybe we work on some skills in high school.

Skills would be great. We were talking about I had a barbershop hand on and she was like, I think we need tax ed. Like we have sex ed, but we really need tax ed. And it's like, yeah, why aren't we teaching the kinds of things, real life skills that we're going to need when these children become adults, which is going to happen in their senior year, and why aren't we preparing these citizens for the real world?

**Kristina:** I agree. And that's one of my things where I want to revamp, reform and just redo education, completely dismantle it. I mean, that would be ideal. But start from the bottom and work our way up because like you said, the kids, not everybody is supposed to or meant to or wants to go to college. And the people that obviously want to or want to become a dentist doctor, absolutely. And we should have that. I mean, some kids know. They know by senior year.

And I think it would be great to have a little dental school 101 or what to expect or something like that. And then for those that don't want to, that last year can be a vocational school, whether it's for plumbers or mechanics, or how to start your business, business entrepreneurship, maybe have a gardening class, maybe a manners class would be nice. Etiquette, manners, that has been lost completely.

**Aimee:** Or soft skills, soft skills too, which are going to be absolutely essential as AI takes everything over, soft skills are going to be more and more important in building really being able to build relationships with people that will then be essential to your career there's so many things we could be teaching and time is really this asset it's this valuable currency that we actually have and where we decide to focus the attention and the intention for the education of these young people it's going to shape their lives right this is crucial time in their lives that we have them captive in this for these years.

And what are we going to how can we help them grow up to be successful citizens of America? Right. That's number one. And I mean, I heard schools are similar to jails. And I was thinking, hmm, that's really interesting. So I started doing some research. I'm like, wait a minute. When the bell rings, they sit at a designated place outside at a table and they eat slop food. That's exactly what we have, slop food. I mean, I've seen what my kids, I remember during COVID, they would bring it home and I'm like, what is this? And it was like a rubber chicken leg. It was not even real. It was just gross.

And then you have this about, oh, organic, why? Why do we have organic? Why don't we just, all our food should be natural and healthy and good for you and organic. It's like we pay this premium to have organic when it's from the earth. And I feel like when we revamp the school system, we need to teach them about foods and chemicals and toxins and how to plant their own food and how to make food. I mean, I would have loved to have home ec. I didn't. I don't know how to sew a button because they didn't have that class.

**Aimee:** Yeah. It's one of those things you actually need. It's a real life skill that you need. You need to know how to do a basic stitch in case your pants get ripped.

**Kristina:** Exactly.

**Aimee:** Well, when you're out or you lose a button. Yeah. These are, I'm so lucky. My mother and my grandmother are both very good at sewing. So I learned those little skills and I did get home ec in junior high, not in high school, but I do think we've lost a lot of the basics. I know when, before I was in high school, there was auto shop at high school. There was home ec at high school and it's how do we get back to where we were and then even better.

We don't know what's coming in the future with AI. We don't really know exactly how to prepare these young people that will be going out into this new brave new world. But we do know that skills are going to be more important than ever. Vocation is going to be more important than ever. What do you think?

**Kristina:** Well, like I said, once I win state Senate, I plan to make legislation just about that, about education. I mean, as a mom of three, I'm running on basically, I'm running on a lot of things for my platform, but it's parental rights, property rights and public safety.
